name: Dandelion
fact: Every part of a dandelion is edible — leaves, flowers, and even the roots.

name: Daisy
fact: Daisies close their petals at night and reopen at dawn — the name comes from 'day's eye'.

name: Sunflower
fact: Young sunflowers track the sun across the sky each day, a behavior called heliotropism.

name: Marigold
fact: Marigolds are natural pest repellents — gardeners plant them around vegetables to keep insects away.

name: Tulip
fact: In 1630s Holland, a single tulip bulb could cost more than a house — peak 'Tulip Mania'.

name: Daffodil
fact: Daffodils contain a toxin called lycorine that wildlife avoids — making them naturally deer- and rodent-proof.

name: Cosmo
fact: Cosmos got their name from the Greek word for harmony, after the orderly arrangement of their petals.

name: Petunia
fact: Petunias are technically related to tomatoes — both belong to the Solanaceae nightshade family.

name: Zinnia
fact: Zinnias were the first flowers grown in space — astronauts cultivated them aboard the ISS in 2016.

name: Dahlia
fact: Dahlias are the national flower of Mexico, where Aztecs grew them as food long before they became ornamentals.

name: Peony
fact: Peonies can live for over 100 years — there are documented plants in China still blooming after 200+ years.

name: Coneflower
fact: Coneflowers (Echinacea) have been used in herbal medicine for centuries to support the immune system.

name: Impatiens
fact: Touch a ripe impatiens seedpod and it'll burst, scattering seeds — the genus name literally means 'impatient'.

name: Pansy
fact: Pansies are edible flowers with a mild minty flavor often used to garnish desserts and salads.

name: Mum
fact: Chrysanthemums (mums) are Japan's imperial flower — the Imperial Seal of Japan is a stylized chrysanthemum.

name: Rose
fact: Roses are over 35 million years old — fossil evidence places them long before humans evolved.

name: Snapdragon
fact: Snapdragons get their name from squeezing the bloom open like a tiny dragon's mouth that snaps shut.

name: Lavender
fact: Lavender belongs to the mint family and bees absolutely love it — a single plant can host hundreds at once.

name: Lily
fact: Some lily varieties are highly toxic to cats — even the pollen can cause kidney failure if ingested.

name: Fuchsia
fact: The color 'fuchsia' was named after the flower, which was named after botanist Leonhart Fuchs in the 1700s.

name: Sweet Peas
fact: Sweet peas are prized for their fragrance, but unlike garden peas they're toxic — never eat them.

name: Hydrangea
fact: Hydrangea flowers change color based on soil pH: acidic soil turns them blue, alkaline soil turns them pink.

name: Gardenia
fact: Gardenia flowers are so fragrant that perfumers describe their scent as 'so heavy it's almost edible'.

name: Hyacinth
fact: Hyacinths are named after a Greek mythological figure beloved by the god Apollo.

name: Poppy
fact: A single poppy plant can produce up to 60,000 tiny seeds in one season.

name: Freesia
fact: Freesia flowers are so prized for their citrus-like scent that they're a staple of high-end perfume blends.

name: Orchid
fact: Orchids form the largest family of flowering plants on Earth — over 28,000 wild species and counting.

name: Blue Poppy
fact: Himalayan blue poppies are notoriously hard to grow — they need cool, damp summers found in only a few regions.

name: Bat Flower
fact: The black bat flower's bloom can reach 12 inches across, with whisker-like bracts trailing up to 28 inches long.

name: Chocolate Cosmo
fact: Chocolate cosmos really do smell like chocolate when they bloom — and they're nearly extinct in the wild.

name: Verbena
fact: Ancient Romans considered verbena a sacred herb of altars, calling it 'the plant of grace'.

name: Bluebells
fact: Bluebell woods can take over a century to establish — they're considered a sign of ancient, undisturbed forest.

name: Honeywort
fact: Honeywort produces so much nectar that bees can drain a flower clean in seconds — its name comes from this trait.

name: Vinca
fact: Vinca (periwinkle) is the source of vincristine, a real-world cancer drug derived from the plant's alkaloids.

name: Passiflora
fact: Passion flowers (Passiflora) were named by Spanish missionaries who saw the crucifixion of Christ in the bloom's structure.
